mappings.rationaleAI: Directly examines how the nature of warfare has changed from Clausewitz's classical framework through nuclear deterrence and asymmetric conflict, addressing degrees and kinds of war including ethnic, regional, and limited wars (E.1) and evolving military strategy (E.2.a).

On War is the foundational Western text on military strategy (E.2.a) and tactics (E.2.b), articulating the Clausewitzian Trinity linking war to politics and establishing the theoretical framework for understanding war among states (E.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: Sun Tzu and The Art of War
mappings.rationaleAI: The Art of War is a foundational text on military strategy (E.2.a), logistics (E.2.c), and espionage and intelligence gathering (D), covering deception, terrain, and the relationship between warfare and statecraft.

A paradigmatic regional and religious war involving multiple states (E.1), concluded by the Peace of Westphalia which established foundational principles of state sovereignty (B.2), with devastating psychological and civilian effects illustrating the impact of prolonged warfare (E.2.d).

An ideologically driven civil war that became an international proxy conflict with German, Italian, and Soviet intervention (E.1), featuring professional armies, international volunteer brigades, and foreign mercenary forces representing diverse types of soldiers (E.3.a).

Directly examines the just war tradition from Augustine through Aquinas to modern international law governing the treatment of persons and moral conduct during wartime (E.2.e), and the criteria for distinguishing legitimate from illegitimate armed conflict (E.1).

The 1884-1885 conference established international rules for claiming and partitioning African territory (B.4) through multilateral diplomatic negotiation among European powers (C.2), directly shaping colonial boundaries and sovereignty.

The 1814-1815 settlement is a paradigmatic treaty terminating war (B.2) that established collective security through great-power concert (B.1), redrew European territorial boundaries (B.4), and pioneered multilateral diplomatic negotiation and arbitration (C.2).

The Battle of Kadesh and subsequent Egyptian-Hittite treaty represent one of history's earliest documented peace agreements terminating hostilities between states (B.2), with Hittite cuneiform archives revealing early diplomacy (C.2) and innovations in chariot warfare illustrating the historical development of military forces (E.3.b).

A national war between two states triggered by territorial disputes (E.1), concluded by a treaty transferring approximately half of Mexico's territory to the United States, exemplifying treaties regarding the sale or transfer of land and settlement of border disputes (B.4).

Ricardo's Theory of Comparative Advantage provided the intellectual foundation for economic and commercial treaties promoting free trade (B.5), and his argument that trade reduces the likelihood of war connects economics to foreign policy aims and international peace (C.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Economic Consequences of the Peace
mappings.rationaleAI: Keynes's critique of the Versailles Treaty directly addresses war-terminating treaties (B.2) and their failure to establish collective security (B.1), while the Paris Peace Conference exemplifies public international diplomacy and negotiation of disputes (C.2).

The 1778 treaties of amity and military cooperation constitute a nonaggression and collective security alliance (B.1) negotiated through diplomacy (C.2), and French naval power at Yorktown illustrates the role of naval forces in deciding interstate conflicts (E.3.c.ii).

Assesses United States foreign policy aims and their compatibility with national and humanitarian interests (C.1), including Cold War interventionism, support for authoritarian regimes, and the global spread of democratic ideals as a transnational political movement (A.1).

Surveys the philosophy of warfare from antiquity through modernity, covering just war theory and international law relating to wartime conduct (E.2.e), Clausewitz's doctrine linking war to politics (E.2.a), and the evolving classification of armed conflict (E.1).

The Franco-Prussian War siege illustrates national warfare between states and the civil insurrection of the Paris Commune (E.1), siege tactics and military operations (E.2.b), and the psychological and civilian effects of wartime deprivation (E.2.d).

Korea's brief assertion of sovereignty involved diplomatic outreach to rival great powers (C.1) before Japan's annexation in 1910, illustrating how territorial treaties and geopolitical competition determine the fate of smaller states (B.4).

The Chinese Communist military campaign during the civil war exemplifies guerrilla warfare and civil war as forms of armed conflict (E.1), with Mao's strategic decisions during the march illustrating military strategy under conditions of retreat and attrition (E.2.a).

The Hanseatic League negotiated tax exemptions, trading monopolies, and diplomatic privileges across sovereign territories (B.5), functioning as an international commercial organization that used collective naval power and diplomacy to protect trade routes (C.2).

The largest anticolonial insurrection in French Algeria between the initial conquest and the 1954 war of independence exemplifies colonial insurrection and guerrilla warfare as a form of armed conflict between a subject population and an imperial state (E.1).

The 1122 Concordat resolved the Investiture Controversy through negotiated treaty (B.2), establishing a diplomatic precedent for separating spheres of authority between competing powers through arbitration and mediation (C.2).

The gold standard functioned as an international monetary framework facilitating economic and commercial treaties and stable exchange rates among nations (B.5), with its collapse during the interwar period directly connected to the breakdown of international economic cooperation.

As Elizabeth I's chief minister, Cecil directed England's foreign policy to defend national security against foreign threats (C.1), and his role in intelligence regarding Mary Queen of Scots exemplifies the use of intelligence activities in preserving national security (D).

The nation state as the fundamental unit of the international system underpins international relations, and the episode examines sovereignty, European integration, and devolution as transnational political movements (A.1) and foreign policy challenges posed by globalization (C.1).

The Risorgimento was a transnational nationalist movement (A.1) that achieved Italian unification through military campaigns against foreign-dominated territories, exemplifying national wars and insurrections (E.1).

The simultaneous revolutionary upheavals across Europe represent civil wars and insurrections driven by liberal and nationalist causes (E.1), constituting a pan-European transnational political movement for self-determination and constitutional reform (A.1).

A decade-long civil war featuring guerrilla warfare by Villa's northern forces and Zapata's agrarian insurgency exemplifies civil wars, insurrections, and guerrilla warfare as forms of armed conflict (E.1).

Oaths cemented peace treaties between warring states and underpinned collective security agreements in the ancient world (B.1), while the Roman military sacramentum illustrates the binding obligations of professional and conscripted soldiers (E.3.a).

The episode discusses the UN Declaration of Human Rights and universal vs cultural rights within the framework of international organizations (A.2), cultural exchange treaties and agreements (B.7), and foreign policy considerations around globalisation's impact on rights (C.3).
